nativeRange.habitat: The Ornate Bichir inhabits calm, shallow freshwater environments — swamps, marshes, floodplains, slow rivers, and the margins of lakes — typically over fine substrates. These habitats are often poorly oxygenated, which is why the species relies on breathing atmospheric air at the surface rather than depending solely on dissolved oxygen in the water.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — abrasive gravel damages belly)
smooth water-worn rocks and spindly driftwood arranged into caves
dense planting with tall, robust species (Vallisneria, large Anubias, Aponogeton)
dim or diffused lighting (shade-tolerant cover plants help)
gentle filtration output — strong currents are avoided in the wild
tight-fitting lid with all gaps sealed (foam/mesh) to prevent escape

diet.notes: The Ornate Bichir is strictly carnivorous and hunts primarily by smell, as its eyesight is poor. Live or frozen meaty foods work best: earthworms, prawns, mussels, krill, bloodworms, chopped fish fillet, and lancefish are all accepted. Smaller specimens tend to do well on brine shrimp and bloodworms, while larger individuals generally need chunkier prey to satisfy them. Some settled specimens can be trained to accept high-quality sinking carnivore pellets. Adults typically feed two to three times per week rather than daily. The Ornate Bichir is a messy eater, so uneaten food should be removed within an hour of feeding to prevent ammonia spikes; strong biological filtration is essential in any setup housing this species.

goodWith: other large Polypterus species of similar size
large African cichlids (Hemichromis, Haplochromis, Tilapia, Severum-type)
medium-to-large characins too big to swallow
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth (will be eaten overnight)
fin-nipping species (rasboras, tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
aggressive cichlids that target the bichir's soft belly
Senegal Bichir (P. senegalus) unless tank is very large — size mismatch risk

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Bloopityboop. 4032x2410. Sharp full lateral view of a vibrant adult showing the diagnostic marbled black-on-cream body, dorsal finlet row and the striking barred yellow-and-black pectoral fin; whole fish in frame, planted tank.

Photographer: Logan McGuire-Warden. 800x587. Different individual; clear lateral view of the reticulated black-on-cream pattern, dorsal finlets and yellow-and-black pectoral marking. Lower resolution, kept for variety.

Photographer: Bloopityboop. 4032x3024. Sharp head-and-shoulder close-up highlighting the diagnostic concentric yellow-and-black rings of the pectoral fin base and marbled head; tight crop so non-recommended.

summary: The Ornate Bichir is the largest of the 'upper-jaw' Polypterus group, reaching 60 cm with a striking bold cream-and-black banded pattern. A living fossil from an ancient Devonian-era lineage, it is an obligate air-breather, highly escape-prone, and a nocturnal carnivore requiring a very large, tightly covered tank.
careNotes: The Ornate Bichir (Polypterus ornatipinnis) belongs to family Polypteridae, one of the oldest ray-finned fish lineages, with fossil relatives tracing back to the Devonian period — making the group hundreds of millions of years old. Rather than relying solely on gills, the species retains paired, lung-like swim bladders with which it breathes atmospheric air; it is an obligate air-breather and MUST have unobstructed access to the water surface at all times. A sealed lid that traps the fish below with no air gap will cause it to drown. The tank lid must be fully secured, with any gaps where cables or tubing enter plugged with fine mesh or filter foam, as bichirs can squeeze through surprisingly small openings and are capable of surviving considerable time out of water. Tank size: a minimum 650 L (approximately 180×60×60 cm) is required for a single adult; adding additional Polypterus species will each increase bioload significantly. Use fine sand substrate exclusively — gravel or coarse substrate abrades the belly and can cause chronic ulceration. Lighting should be low or heavily diffused, as the species is strictly nocturnal and spends daylight hours sheltering in caves. Feeding after lights-out generally produces the best response. Water quality: this species produces substantial waste from its high-protein diet, making efficient biological filtration non-negotiable. Nitrates should be kept below 20 ppm, and ammonia spikes after feeding are a real risk — uneaten food should be siphoned out within an hour of feeding. Despite its prehistoric appearance, the Ornate Bichir tends to be relatively peaceful toward tankmates too large to swallow; conspecific and same-genus tankmates are generally tolerated when size-matched. Its copper sensitivity means it is incompatible with copper-based treatments applied to the main display tank. See also: Senegal Bichir ([fw-fish-127]) — a smaller, more beginner-friendly species in the same genus.
breedingNotes: The Ornate Bichir is rarely bred in home aquaria, and most specimens in the trade are wild-caught. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately five to six years of age. Spawning is triggered by simulating rainy-season conditions: lowering the temperature slightly, reducing pH toward 6.5, and performing large water changes with slightly cooler water. During spawning, the male cups his anal fin around the female's genital region and fertilises eggs externally; the adhesive eggs scatter among fine-leafed plants. A single spawn may produce up to 300 eggs. Parents will consume the eggs if left in the tank and should be removed promptly after spawning concludes. Eggs hatch in approximately three to four days at 26°C; the larvae bear external gills (which are gradually reabsorbed over the first few weeks) and become free-swimming after absorbing the yolk sac. Fry are fed baby brine shrimp and micro-worms, and juveniles grow quickly enough that size-sorting will be necessary to prevent larger individuals from preying on smaller ones.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus ornatapinnis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size 60 cm, temperature 77–82°F (25–28°C), pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–25°H, minimum tank 650 L (180×60×60 cm), native range (Cameroon, DRC, Angola, Burundi, Tanzania), habitat, diet (live/frozen meaty foods; dislikes dried), compatible species list, nocturnal/escape-risk behaviour, and breeding trigger notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Polypterus ornatipinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (60 cm TL), temperature 26–28°C, native range (Congo basin, Lake Tanganyika, Lake Rukwa drainage), taxonomy (Polypteridae, Polypteriformes, class Cladistii), diet (worms and insect larvae when young; mainly fish when adult), demersal freshwater habitat, trophic level 3.7, dorsal spines 9–11.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Ornate Bichir
additional.notes: Confirmed water parameters (pH 6.9–8.0, hardness up to 25 dH, temperature 77–82°F), minimum tank 540 L (6×2×2 ft), captive adult size ~58 cm, breeding behaviour (male anal-fin embrace, adhesive eggs, 3–4 day hatch), and feeding details (frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, earthworms, prawns, mussels, krill; sinking pellets accepted when soaked).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Ornate Bichir
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-care guide. Confirmed lifespan 10–20 years, copper sensitivity, escape risk (primitive lungs allow extended land survival), obligate air-breathing note, sexual maturity at 5–6 years, and up to 300 eggs per spawn. Minimum tank listed as 180 gallons (~680 L).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Polypterus ornatipinnis
additional.notes: Confirmed 9–11 dorsal spines, black-and-yellow patterning, 60 cm maximum length, Lake Tanganyika and Congo River basin range, obligate air-breather (divided swim bladder), and living fossil context (Polypteridae relatives dating to ancient actinopterygian stem lineage).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is uncommon and most Ornate Bichirs in the trade are wild-caught. Spawning can be induced by simulating rainy-season conditions — dropping the temperature slightly, nudging pH toward 6.5, and performing large water changes with slightly cooler water. The male fertilises eggs externally by cupping his anal fin around the female's genital region; up to 300 adhesive eggs are scattered among fine-leafed plants. Eggs hatch in three to four days, and the larvae initially bear external gills that are reabsorbed over the following weeks. Parents should be removed from the breeding tank as soon as spawning is complete, as they will otherwise consume the eggs.
